nodes.name: Plays & Playwrights
nodes.description: Dramatic works and the writers who created them

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek tragedian wrote over 120 plays, won 24 of 30 competitions at the Dionysia, and created *Oedipus Rex* and *Antigone*?
items.explanation:
Ancient Greek tragedian (c. 496–406 BC), born at Colonus near Athens
Wrote over 120 plays; only 7 survive complete: *Ajax*, *Antigone*, *Women of Trachis*, *Oedipus Rex*, *Electra*, *Philoctetes*, *Oedipus at Colonus*
Competed in 30 dramatic competitions, won 24, never placed lower than second
Aristotle used *Oedipus Rex* in his *Poetics* as the model of perfect tragedy
Introduced the third speaking actor to Greek drama, expanding dialogue beyond two performers
Served as a state treasurer (hellenotamias) and as a general alongside Pericles in the campaign against Samos (c. 441 BC)
Died in 406 BC at roughly age 90; Euripides died the same year

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek tragedian, the last of Athens's three great tragic dramatists, wrote *Medea* and *The Bacchae*, and had 18 surviving plays — more than any other Greek tragedian?
items.explanation:
Ancient Greek tragedian (c. 484–406 BC), the youngest of the three great Athenian tragedians after Aeschylus and Sophocles
Wrote roughly 90 plays; 18 survive complete — more than any other Greek tragedian
*Medea* (431 BC) depicts a wife's revenge after being abandoned by Jason of Argonauts fame
*The Bacchae* was produced posthumously; his son staged it after his death in Macedonia
*Hippolytus* (428 BC) won first prize at the City Dionysia — one of only four or five first-prize victories in his career
Known for psychologically complex characters, especially women, and for questioning traditional mythology and religion
Died in Macedonia at the court of King Archelaus in 406 BC, the same year Sophocles died in Athens

items.prompt: Which Norwegian playwright, called the 'father of modern drama,' wrote *A Doll's House* and *Hedda Gabler* and was the most performed dramatist in the world after Shakespeare as of 2014?
items.explanation:
Norwegian playwright and theatre director (1828–1906)
Often called the 'father of modern drama' or 'father of realism' for pioneering realistic prose drama on social themes
*A Doll's House* (1879) shocked audiences with Nora's decision to leave her husband and children, sparking debate across Europe
*Hedda Gabler* (1891) features one of theatre's most complex female protagonists
Other major plays include *Peer Gynt* (1867), *An Enemy of the People* (1882), *The Wild Duck* (1884), and *Ghosts* (1881)
As of 2014, the most frequently performed dramatist in the world after Shakespeare
Lived in self-imposed exile from Norway for 27 years (1864–1891), writing most of his major works in Italy and Germany

items.prompt: Which Russian playwright and short-story master wrote *The Cherry Orchard*, *Three Sisters*, and *Uncle Vanya*, all premiered by the Moscow Art Theatre?
items.explanation:
Russian playwright and short-story writer (1860–1904)
Four major plays — *The Seagull* (1896), *Uncle Vanya* (1899), *Three Sisters* (1901), *The Cherry Orchard* (1904) — all staged by Konstantin Stanislavski's Moscow Art Theatre
*The Seagull* flopped at its 1896 premiere but was revived to acclaim by the Moscow Art Theatre in 1898, saving both the play and the theatre
Also a physician; famously said 'Medicine is my lawful wife and literature is my mistress'
Married actress Olga Knipper in 1901; they lived mostly apart — he in Yalta for his health, she in Moscow for her career
Died of tuberculosis at age 44 in the German spa town of Badenweiler; his last words reportedly were 'I haven't had champagne for a long time'
Pioneered a style of drama where mood, subtext, and inaction replaced traditional plot-driven structure
items.cardFront: Russian, early 1900s — *The Cherry Orchard*, *Three Sisters*, *Uncle Vanya*

items.prompt: Which Irish wit and aesthete wrote the comedies *The Importance of Being Earnest* and *An Ideal Husband*, both premiering in London in 1895 before his imprisonment?
items.explanation:
Irish playwright, poet, and author (1854–1900)
Full name: Oscar Fingal O'Fflahertie Wills Wilde
*The Importance of Being Earnest* (premiered February 14, 1895) is widely considered the greatest comedy of manners in English
*An Ideal Husband* opened January 3, 1895, at the Haymarket Theatre; the Prince of Wales attended opening night
At the peak of his fame in 1895, convicted of 'gross indecency' for homosexual acts and sentenced to two years' hard labor at Reading Gaol
Also wrote the novel *The Picture of Dorian Gray* (1890) and the play *Lady Windermere's Fan* (1892)
Died penniless in Paris at age 46; reportedly said on his deathbed, 'This wallpaper and I are fighting a duel to the death. One of us has got to go'
items.cardFront: Irish, 1890s — *The Importance of Being Earnest*, *An Ideal Husband*

items.prompt: Which Irish playwright wrote over 60 plays including *Pygmalion* and *Saint Joan*, won the 1925 Nobel Prize in Literature, and was the first person to win both a Nobel and an Oscar?
items.explanation:
Irish playwright, critic, and political activist (1856–1950)
Wrote over 60 plays including *Pygmalion* (1913), *Saint Joan* (1923), *Man and Superman* (1902), and *Major Barbara* (1905)
Awarded the 1925 Nobel Prize in Literature for works 'marked by both idealism and humanity'
First person to win both a Nobel Prize and an Academy Award — the Oscar was for the 1938 film adaptation of *Pygmalion*
*Pygmalion* inspired the musical *My Fair Lady* (1956); Shaw had refused musical adaptations during his lifetime
A committed socialist and prominent member of the Fabian Society, writing many of its pamphlets
Lived to 94; died from complications after falling while pruning a tree in his garden

items.prompt: Which American playwright wrote *Death of a Salesman* and *The Crucible*, won a Pulitzer Prize in 1949, and was called before the House Un-American Activities Committee?
items.explanation:
American playwright (1915–2005)
*Death of a Salesman* opened on Broadway February 10, 1949, directed by Elia Kazan; won the Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the Tony Award for Best Play
*The Crucible* (1953) used the Salem witch trials as an allegory for McCarthyism and the Red Scare
Subpoenaed by the House Un-American Activities Committee in 1956; refused to name names and was convicted of contempt of Congress (later overturned)
Married Marilyn Monroe in 1956; they divorced in 1961
Other major works include *All My Sons* (1947), *A View from the Bridge* (1955), and *After the Fall* (1964)
Considered one of the three foremost American dramatists of the 20th century, alongside Eugene O'Neill and Tennessee Williams
items.cardFront: American, mid-20th c. — *Death of a Salesman*, *The Crucible*

items.prompt: Which American playwright, born Thomas Lanier III in Mississippi, wrote *A Streetcar Named Desire* and *Cat on a Hot Tin Roof*, winning two Pulitzer Prizes for Drama?
items.explanation:
American playwright (1911–1983), born Thomas Lanier Williams III
Won two Pulitzer Prizes for Drama: *A Streetcar Named Desire* (1948) and *Cat on a Hot Tin Roof* (1955)
*The Glass Menagerie* (1944) was his breakthrough; it opened in Chicago in December 1944 and transferred to Broadway in March 1945
*A Streetcar Named Desire* created the iconic role of Stanley Kowalski, which made Marlon Brando a star on stage and screen
Set most of his plays in the American South, exploring themes of desire, loneliness, and decay
Struggled with depression, alcohol, and drug dependence for much of his life
Died in 1983 in a New York hotel room at age 71; reportedly choked on a bottle cap
items.cardFront: American, mid-20th c. — *A Streetcar Named Desire*, *Cat on a Hot Tin Roof*

items.prompt: Which Irish expatriate, living in Paris, wrote *Waiting for Godot* and *Endgame*, won the 1969 Nobel Prize in Literature, and originally composed many of his works in French?
items.explanation:
Irish playwright, novelist, and poet (1906–1989), based in Paris from the 1930s onward
*Waiting for Godot* (premiered January 5, 1953, Théâtre de Babylone, Paris) — originally written in French as *En attendant Godot*; considered the most significant play of the 20th century
Key figure in the Theatre of the Absurd alongside Eugène Ionesco and Jean Genet
Awarded the 1969 Nobel Prize in Literature 'for his writing, which—in new forms for the novel and drama—in the destitution of modern man acquires its elevation'
Joined the French Resistance during World War II; his cell was infiltrated and he fled to Roussillon for the war's duration
Wrote many works first in French, then translated them into English himself
Other major plays include *Endgame* (1957), *Krapp's Last Tape* (1958), and *Happy Days* (1961)

items.prompt: Which American playwright wrote *Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?* and won three Pulitzer Prizes for Drama — for *A Delicate Balance*, *Seascape*, and *Three Tall Women*?
items.explanation:
American playwright (1928–2016)
*Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?* (1962) — a searing portrait of a bitter academic marriage — was selected for the 1963 Pulitzer but overruled by the advisory board, which gave no drama prize that year
Won three Pulitzer Prizes for Drama: *A Delicate Balance* (1967), *Seascape* (1975), and *Three Tall Women* (1994)
*The Zoo Story* (1958) was his first play, a one-act that premiered in Berlin before New York
Adopted at two weeks old by Reed and Frances Albee; his adoptive grandfather was vaudeville magnate Edward Franklin Albee II
Known for stripping away social pretense to expose raw emotion and marital dysfunction
The 1966 film of *Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?* starred Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton, earning five Academy Awards
items.cardFront: American — *Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf?*, three Pulitzers

items.prompt: Which Czech-born British playwright wrote *Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead*, won an Oscar for co-writing *Shakespeare in Love*, and was knighted in 1997?
items.explanation:
Czech-born British playwright and screenwriter (1937–2025), born Tomáš Straussler in Zlín, Czechoslovakia
*Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead* (1966) retells *Hamlet* from the perspective of two minor characters; won the Tony Award for Best Play
*Arcadia* (1993) interweaves 19th-century and modern timelines in an English country house, exploring chaos theory and Romanticism
Co-wrote the screenplay for *Shakespeare in Love* (1998) with Marc Norman; won the Academy Award for Best Original Screenplay
*The Coast of Utopia* trilogy (2002) about 19th-century Russian intellectuals won the Tony for Best Play
Knighted by Queen Elizabeth II in 1997 for services to literature
Died November 29, 2025, in Dorset, England, at age 88
items.cardFront: British-Czech — *Rosencrantz and Guildenstern Are Dead*, *Shakespeare in Love*

items.prompt: Which American playwright created the Pittsburgh Cycle — ten plays chronicling the African-American experience across each decade of the 20th century — including *Fences* and *The Piano Lesson*?
items.explanation:
American playwright (1945–2005), born in the Hill District of Pittsburgh
The Pittsburgh Cycle (also called the Century Cycle) comprises ten plays, one set in each decade of the 20th century, chronicling the African-American experience
Won two Pulitzer Prizes for Drama: *Fences* (1987) and *The Piano Lesson* (1990)
*Fences* also won the Tony Award for Best Play; the 2016 film starred Denzel Washington and Viola Davis
*Ma Rainey's Black Bottom* (1984) was his first Broadway play; adapted as a 2020 Netflix film with Chadwick Boseman's final role
Completed the final play, *Radio Golf* (set in the 1990s), shortly before his death in 2005
The August Wilson Theatre on Broadway (renamed 2005) is one of the few Broadway houses named for an African-American
items.cardFront: American — *Fences*, Pittsburgh Cycle (10 plays, one per decade)

items.prompt: Which British playwright, known for loaded silences and 'comedy of menace,' wrote *The Birthday Party* and *The Homecoming* and won the 2005 Nobel Prize in Literature?
items.explanation:
British playwright, screenwriter, director, and actor (1930–2008)
Awarded the 2005 Nobel Prize in Literature for work that 'uncovers the precipice under everyday prattle and forces entry into oppression's closed rooms'
*The Birthday Party* (1958) flopped on its London premiere but was later recognized as a masterpiece of modern drama
*The Homecoming* (1965) won the Tony Award for Best Play when it transferred to Broadway in 1967
Coined the theatrical concept of the 'Pinter pause' — loaded silences that carry as much meaning as dialogue
Also a prolific screenwriter; credits include *The Servant* (1963), *The Go-Between* (1971), and *The French Lieutenant's Woman* (1981)
Became an outspoken political activist in later life, vocally opposing the Iraq War and U.S. foreign policy

items.prompt: Which American playwright won the 1936 Nobel Prize in Literature and four Pulitzer Prizes for Drama, including one posthumously for *Long Day's Journey Into Night*?
items.explanation:
American playwright (1888–1953), the only dramatist to win four Pulitzer Prizes for Drama
Pulitzer wins: *Beyond the Horizon* (1920), *Anna Christie* (1922), *Strange Interlude* (1928), and *Long Day's Journey Into Night* (1957, posthumous)
Awarded the 1936 Nobel Prize in Literature — the first American playwright so honored
*Long Day's Journey Into Night* is autobiographical, depicting his own family's dysfunction; he stipulated it not be published until 25 years after his death, but his widow Carlotta released it in 1956
*The Iceman Cometh* (1946) and *Mourning Becomes Electra* (1931) are among his other landmark works
Introduced European expressionism and psychological realism to American theatre, elevating it from melodrama to serious art
Died in a Boston hotel room in 1953; his last words reportedly were 'Born in a hotel room, and — God damn it — died in a hotel room'
items.cardFront: American — *Long Day's Journey Into Night*, Nobel 1936, 4 Pulitzers

items.prompt: Which American playwright wrote *Angels in America: A Gay Fantasia on National Themes*, a two-part epic about the AIDS crisis that won the Pulitzer Prize and consecutive Tony Awards in 1993 and 1994?
items.explanation:
American playwright and screenwriter (born 1956)
*Angels in America* is a two-part play: *Millennium Approaches* (1991) and *Perestroika* (1992)
*Millennium Approaches* won the 1993 Pulitzer Prize for Drama; both parts won consecutive Tony Awards for Best Play (1993 and 1994)
The play interweaves the AIDS epidemic, Mormonism, McCarthyism, and the Reagan era through interconnected storylines in 1980s New York
A 2003 HBO miniseries directed by Mike Nichols starred Al Pacino and Meryl Streep; won 11 Emmy Awards
Also co-wrote the screenplays for Steven Spielberg's *Munich* (2005) and *Lincoln* (2012)
Considered the most important American play of the late 20th century; frequently revived, including a 2018 Broadway production starring Andrew Garfield
items.cardFront: American — *Angels in America*, Pulitzer, consecutive Tonys 1993–1994
